title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds to Oklahoma Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat's media claim that SB13 is a secession bill, arguing it is a nullification bill no different from measures Treat himself has previously sponsored or supported. The piece exposes Treat's hypocrisy and accuses him of slandering abolitionists to avoid politically costly action against abortion.

questions.question: What is Greg Treat's role in opposing SB13, and how has he used his position to block it?
questions.answer: Treat, as Senate Pro Tem, has been the primary obstacle to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act across two sessions, both publicly attacking SB13 in the media and using his power inside the chamber to keep the bill from being heard.
questions.quote: Oklahoma State Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat has been the primary opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act over the past two legislative sessions. Since the Abolition Day rally last year, Treat has gone to the media to denounce SB13, authored bills designed to be substitutes for SB13, and instructed former Senator Jason Smalley and Senator Greg McCortney not to hear SB13.

questions.question: What was Treat's 2019 line of attack against SB13, and why did abolitionists consider it hypocritical?
questions.answer: In 2019 Treat denounced nullification as villainous, but abolitionists pointed out that he himself sponsored or voted for nullification measures on marijuana decriminalization and EPA regulations, undermining his stated principle.
questions.quote: In 2018, he voted in favor of putting the decriminalization of marijuana on the ballot, a violation of the federal Controlled Substances Act and the supreme court's decision in *Gonzales v. Raich*. In 2016, he not only voted in favor of but was the primary sponsor of SB1379 which nullified federal EPA regulations.

questions.question: How did Treat reframe his opposition to SB13 in 2020, and where did he make these claims?
questions.answer: After being exposed on nullification, Treat shifted to characterizing SB13 as a quasi-secession bill in interviews with the Norman Transcript and The Oklahoman.
questions.quote: To the *Norman Transcript* last week, Treat said "I still am of the mind that [SB13 is] fatally flawed, and I believe that in large part that's a bill to secede from the union more than it is to save lives."

questions.question: Who actually backs SB13, and why does Silberman list them?
questions.answer: A coalition of educational organizations, two Baptist denominations, the state Republican Party platform, and thousands of grassroots Oklahomans support SB13, demonstrating that the motivation is love of preborn neighbors rather than a fringe secessionist agenda.
questions.quote: Free the States, The Ekklesia of Oklahoma, and Oklahomans United for Life are three political-educational organizations which support SB13. Two Christian denominations, the Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists and Oklahoma Baptists (Southern Baptist Convention), both passed resolutions in support of SB13 in the past nine months. The Oklahoma State Republican Party amended their platform to call for abortion's total and immediate abolition last year as well.

questions.question: What does SB13 actually say that Treat is calling secession, and how does the article characterize it?
questions.answer: Section 33 declares federal actions purporting to override the Act void under both state and U.S. Constitutions; Silberman argues this is straightforward nullification, no different in kind from the marijuana and EPA measures Treat himself supported.
questions.quote: Section 33 establishes that "Any federal statute, regulation, executive order or court decision which purports to supersede, stay or overrule this Act is in violation of the Constitution of the State of Oklahoma and the Constitution of the United States of America and is therefore void." This is no more a call to secession than Treat's SB1379 or the decriminalization of medical marijuana were examples of secession.

questions.question: Why does Silberman accuse Treat of slander rather than honest disagreement?
questions.answer: Silberman argues Treat is misrepresenting the motives of thousands of abolitionists, casting their love for the preborn as a cynical cover for a secessionist agenda, in order to dodge the courageous action required of him.
questions.quote: Treat seems to want you to believe that the motivation of all the work being done by all these various people and organizations is a desire to secede from the union rather than love for our preborn neighbors, and that we merely pretend to love our preborn neighbors while using them as a political tool to accomplish our actual objective.

questions.question: What does the article say about the broader pro-life Republican establishment's use of the abortion issue?
questions.answer: Silberman argues that the pro-life political establishment treats abortion as a means to win elections rather than an injustice to actually end, and accuses Treat of doing the same through ongoing disinformation about SB13.
questions.quote: "The life issue" is a means to an end for the Republican pro-life establishment. They pretend like they're doing something about abortion to appease their constituents and get reelected but show no desire to actually put an end to the slaughter.

questions.question: How does Free the States frame its own motives in response to Treat's accusations?
questions.answer: The organization frames its work as a single-minded pursuit of justice for the preborn done in the name of Jesus Christ, and pledges that slander will not deter it or its allied organizations and activists.
questions.quote: But as for the accusations, not a single staff or board member of Free the States has any objective in mind other than establishing justice for those who are currently being murdered under the cover of Oklahoma State Law, and doing so in the name of and for the glory of Jesus Christ.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Directly rebuts Treat's 2019 anti-nullification framing referenced in this article.

related.reason: Extends the slander/disinformation argument applied here against Treat to broader anti-SB13 messaging.

related.reason: Provides the positive case for SB13 that Treat's substitute-bill strategy is trying to undercut.

related.reason: Background on the nullification doctrine that SB13's Section 33 invokes and that Treat misrepresents.
